content.claim: Hanushek & Woessmann's difference-in-differences analysis using TIMSS/PIRLS/PISA data finds that early tracking increases educational inequality and shows a tendency to reduce mean performance, with Hungary named as an early-tracking case.
content.context: Fresh live research through the International comparison lens
limitations: Explicitly contested by subsequent meta-analyses: a 75-country replication found inconclusive effects on social inequality and noted that international comparative studies operate at small country-level sample sizes (n≈20-75 countries).
Causal identification relies on cross-national differences in tracking age, which are confounded with curriculum, teacher training, and governance structures that change simultaneously.
content.population: Population described in the cited finding
content.time_scope: As reported by the cited source
